content:
The first web scraper I ever wrote lasted exactly one successful run.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by balesstudio on Unsplashread here.
The second run failed.
At first, I assumed I had made a mistake in my Python code. After all, beginners usually blame themselves first. I opened the terminal, reread every function, added more print statements than I’d like to admit, and spent nearly an hour searching for a bug that didn’t exist.
The website had simply changed.
One HTML class name was different.
That was the day I realized something important:
Building a web scraper isn’t about collecting data. It’s about building automation that survives change.
Most tutorials stop after showing you how to extract a few headlines using BeautifulSoup. It feels satisfying because everything works perfectly inside a controlled example.
Real websites don’t care about your tutorial.
They redesign pages.
They introduce rate limits.
They hide data behind JavaScript.
They block suspicious traffic.
